title: Cuts and Scrapes: Basic Wound Care
summary: Learn to recognize a minor scrape you can handle yourself, and when a cut means you stop and get an adult right away.

sections.heading: Blood can carry hidden germs
body: You can't see germs, but some live in blood. A few of them can make a person very sick.

Rule: never touch someone else's blood with your bare hands. If a teammate is bleeding, don't touch the wound yourself — tell a coach, athletic trainer, or school nurse right away. They have gloves and know how to help safely.
If you get a small scrape on your own skin, wash your hands before and after you touch it.

callout.title: Your job with someone else's wound
callout.text: See blood on a teammate? Don't touch it. Get an adult immediately and let them take it from there.

sections.heading: Is it a minor scrape or something bigger?
body: A minor scrape or cut is small, close to the surface of the skin, and only oozes a little blood. Think of a scraped knee from falling on the track, or a small cut from a fingernail.
Anything bigger than that is not something to handle yourself. That includes a deep cut, a wound that is bleeding a lot, or a cut with something stuck in it, like a splinter or a piece of glass.
When you're not sure if a wound is minor, treat it like it isn't. Get an adult to look at it.

image.description: Side-by-side illustration comparing a small, shallow scraped knee with light oozing to a deeper cut on a forearm with a warning-colored outline around it, showing the difference between a wound you can handle yourself and one that needs an adult. Clean, non-graphic illustration style for middle schoolers.
image.alt: Comparison drawing of a minor scraped knee next to a deeper cut marked as needing an adult's attention.

sections.heading: Caring for your own minor scrape
list: If it's bleeding, press a clean cloth or gauze on it and hold it there.
Rinse the scrape gently with clean water to wash away dirt.
Tell an adult about it, even if it seems small — they can check it and help if it needs more care.

image.description: Four small step illustrations in one image: hands being washed at a sink, a clean cloth pressed on a scraped knee, the knee being rinsed with water from a bottle, and the knee covered with a bandage. Clean, simple illustration style for middle schoolers, no gore.
image.alt: Four-step illustration showing washing hands, pressing a cloth on a scrape, rinsing it, and covering it with a bandage.

sections.heading: Get an adult right away
body: Some wounds are never a "handle it yourself" situation, even if they happen to you. These need an adult immediately — no waiting, no trying it on your own first.

callout.title: Stop and get an adult immediately if you see...
callout.text: Blood that is spurting out or won't stop after a few minutes of firm pressure, a deep or gaping cut, anything stuck in the wound (never pull it out), a wound from a bite, a wound from something rusty or dirty, or numbness/inability to move the area.

sections.heading: Watching a bandage heal
body: Keep the bandage clean and dry, and change it if it gets wet or dirty.
Check it each day. If the skin around it turns more red, feels warm, swells up, or has pus, that's a sign of infection — germs growing in the wound. Tell an adult right away so a doctor can check it.
A fever along with a healing scrape is also a reason to tell an adult right away.

image.description: Side-by-side illustration comparing a healing scrape with a fresh bandage and normal-colored skin around it to a scrape with a bandage removed showing red, swollen skin around the edges, marked with a warning-colored outline. Clean, non-graphic illustration style for middle schoolers.
image.alt: Comparison drawing of a normally healing bandaged scrape next to one with red, swollen skin showing signs of infection.

sections.heading: Nosebleeds
body: Sit up and lean slightly forward — don't tilt your head back, because that lets blood run down your throat.
Pinch the soft part of your nose and hold it for a full 10 minutes without letting go to check.
If it doesn't stop after 10 minutes, or it happened after a hit to the head, tell an adult right away.

image.description: Illustration of a student sitting upright and leaning slightly forward, pinching the soft part of their nose with a tissue, clearly not tilting their head back. Clean, simple illustration style for middle schoolers, no gore.
image.alt: Illustration of a student sitting up and leaning slightly forward while pinching their nose for a nosebleed.

quiz.question: You see a teammate bleeding from a cut on their arm. What should you do first?
choices: Tell a coach, athletic trainer, or school nurse right away
Press on it with your bare hands to help
Wrap it in your own jacket
Ignore it since it's not your wound
quiz.explanation: You should never touch someone else's blood with bare hands. Getting an adult immediately means the wound gets handled safely by someone with gloves and training.

quiz.question: Why is it risky to touch someone else's blood with your bare hands?
choices: It might stain your clothes
Blood can carry germs you can't see, and some can make you very sick
It's against the rules of most sports
It could get on your shoes
quiz.explanation: Blood can carry hidden germs that cause serious illness. That's why bare-hand contact with someone else's blood is never okay — leave it to an adult with gloves.

quiz.question: Which of these counts as a minor scrape you could care for yourself?
choices: A deep cut with a piece of glass stuck in it
Blood that keeps spurting out
A small, shallow scrape on your knee that oozes a little blood
A bite wound
quiz.explanation: A small, shallow scrape with light oozing is minor. Deep cuts, embedded objects, spurting blood, and bites all need an adult right away, not self-care.

quiz.question: What is the correct order of steps for caring for your own minor scrape?
choices: Cover it, rinse it, wash your hands
Rinse it with alcohol, wrap it tightly, ignore it
Pull off any loose skin, then cover it
Wash your hands, press on it if bleeding, rinse it, cover it
quiz.explanation: Start with clean hands, control any bleeding with pressure, rinse away dirt with clean water, then cover with a bandage.

quiz.question: You find a splinter stuck deep in your own cut. What should you do?
choices: Leave it alone and tell an adult right away
Pull it out quickly so it doesn't hurt as long
Dig it out carefully with a pin
Cover it with a bandage and forget about it
quiz.explanation: Never pull an object out of a wound yourself. Get an adult immediately — removing it the wrong way can make bleeding worse.

quiz.question: A few days after a scrape, the skin around it looks more red, feels warm, and has pus. What does this mean?
choices: The scrape is healing perfectly
This could be an infection — tell an adult right away
This is normal and needs no attention
You should scrub it harder with soap
quiz.explanation: Spreading redness, warmth, and pus are signs of infection — germs growing in the wound. An adult needs to know so a doctor can check it.

quiz.question: What is the correct way to handle a nosebleed?
choices: Tilt your head back and lie down
Stuff tissue far up the nose and stand still
Sit up, lean slightly forward, and pinch the soft part of your nose for 10 minutes
Blow your nose hard right away
quiz.explanation: Leaning forward keeps blood from running down the throat. Pinching the soft part of the nose for a full 10 minutes stops most nosebleeds.

quiz.question: Which of these means you should get an adult right away, no matter what?
choices: A tiny scrape with barely any blood
A bandage that needs to be changed after gym class
A small bruise from bumping a locker
Blood that won't stop after a few minutes of firm pressure
quiz.explanation: Bleeding that keeps going despite firm pressure is a sign that this is not a minor wound — it needs an adult's help immediately.

flashcards.front: Why can't you touch someone else's blood with bare hands?
flashcards.back: Blood can carry hidden germs that make people very sick. Always get an adult instead.

flashcards.front: What should you do if a teammate is bleeding?
flashcards.back: Don't touch the wound yourself. Tell a coach, athletic trainer, or school nurse right away.
A small, shallow wound close to the surface of the skin that only oozes a little blood.

flashcards.front: First step in caring for your own minor scrape
flashcards.back: Wash your hands before you touch it.

flashcards.front: How do you stop a scrape from bleeding?
flashcards.back: Press a clean cloth or gauze on it and hold it there.

flashcards.front: How do you clean a minor scrape?
flashcards.back: Rinse it gently with clean water to wash away dirt, then cover with a bandage.

flashcards.front: Object stuck in a wound — what do you do?
flashcards.back: Never pull it out yourself. Get an adult right away.

flashcards.front: Name three signs a wound might be infected
flashcards.back: Spreading redness, warmth, and pus around the wound (a fever is also a warning sign).
Sit up and lean slightly forward — never tilt your head back.

flashcards.front: How long do you pinch the nose for a nosebleed?
flashcards.back: A full 10 minutes without letting go, to check if it has stopped.

flashcards.front: Name three wounds that always need an adult, not self-care
flashcards.back: A deep cut, a bite wound, and blood that won't stop after firm pressure.

flashcards.front: When in doubt about a wound...
flashcards.back: Treat it like it's serious. Get an adult to look at it.

sources.title: National Athletic Trainers' Association Position Statement: Management of Acute Skin Trauma
